freedom.label: Freedom hypothesis
freedom.summary: For creative/game implementation tasks, agent performance correlates INVERSELY with framework constraint. Less prescribed structure leads to better output.
evidence: Bare consistently outperforms GAD on human review across rounds 2-4
GAD has never exceeded 0.30 human review across 8+ attempts
Process metrics (planning_quality, discipline) do not correlate with output quality
counter_evidence: Single-run variance is high — not yet statistically significant
GAD may win on context-resumption and multi-phase refactors
Rate limits affected all conditions unevenly
Brownfield experiments may show GAD's overhead pays off for extension

csh.label: Compound-Skills hypothesis
csh.summary: Emergent workflow with inherited + evolved skills improves over rounds as the skill ratchet compounds. Tests whether skill inheritance is a net positive or noise.
evidence: First full skill ratcheting cycle observed in emergent v4
Emergent v4 authored 2 new project-tailored skills and deprecated 1 unusable one
Maintained phase commits under rate-limit pressure (inherited skill discipline)
counter_evidence: Emergent v1 performed worse than bare v2 despite inherited skills
Skills captured patterns, not failure modes — insufficient for KAPLAY API issues
Sample size too small for statistical claims

gad_framework.label: GAD framework
gad_framework.summary: Full GAD framework with .planning/ XML, AGENTS.md loop, and skill triggers. Tests whether structured planning overhead pays off in output quality.
evidence: Perfect process metrics: planning_quality 1.0, full task tracking
Recoverable after context compaction via planning docs
Framework overhead may pay off for long-horizon multi-phase work
counter_evidence: Never exceeded 0.30 human review across 8+ greenfield attempts
Two blank screens (v5, v6), broken game loops (v7, v8)
Planning doc maintenance consumed tokens that could go to implementation
API-interrupted before implementation in v9, v10
